identity.title: Dry Pasta, Dough, and Flour Mixes Manufacturing from Purchased Flour

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers in NAICS 311824 that repeatedly supply dry pasta, prepared flour mixes, or refrigerated or frozen dough to external retail, foodservice, institutional, or food-manufacturer customers, including private-label and customer-specific production.
lens.excluded_activities: Branded manufacturers without a material repeat outsourced-supply relationship, captive plants, shell or financing entities, fresh-pasta manufacturers classified elsewhere, and businesses outside the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring product sales under private-label, institutional, ingredient, foodservice, or customer-specific arrangements; revenue is typically per unit or shipment, with some contracts adjusting prices for input costs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default recurring outsourced-service lens is narrowed to customer-specific, private-label, institutional, ingredient, and foodservice supply because the NAICS code also contains branded consumer-product manufacturers whose principal activity is not an outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 311800 occupation mix, not 311824 or AI exposure. I map the large production, packaging, material-moving, office, and quality-control groups to task-level opportunities: computer vision and decision support can reduce inspection, documentation, scheduling, forecasting, and administrative work, while hands-on sanitation, maintenance, material handling, and line operation remain much less exposed. The broader bakery population likely overstates manual baking and retail roles relative to continuous pasta and mix plants, which may make this estimate conservative for 311824.
a.rationale: Production occupations were 49.70% of broader-industry employment and transportation/material moving was 13.23%, while office support was 5.24%. Within production, packaging-machine operators alone were 6.71% and inspectors 1.14%. A pasta-line example demonstrates real-time machine vision replacing manual defect inspection, but most payroll still supports physical processing, cleaning, maintenance, and movement rather than language-centric work.
a.change_evidence: A 311824 payroll extract by SOC and wage, combined with plant time studies that identify current manual inspection, planning, order-entry, quality-documentation, and changeover tasks and record existing automation, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: OEWS is 2023 and only available at NAICS 311800, which includes bakeries and tortillas and therefore has a materially different occupation mix.
The machine-vision example is a vendor/project account from an Italian production-equipment context, not a representative US adoption study.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated on continuous production and packaging lines.

rho.bridge: The RINA project establishes technical feasibility for in-line pasta inspection, while FDA requirements establish quality, training, hazard-control, monitoring, and recordkeeping constraints. I translate those facts into the share of the exposed opportunity that a typical LMM US plant could implement within five years. A proven but supplier-specific deployment may overstate ease of integration; food-safety obligations and legacy equipment pull the estimate downward.
rho.rationale: Machine vision can identify pasta defects and reject product in real time, and software can support administrative and planning workflows. Implementation is restrained by capital availability, legacy-line integration, validation, customer specifications, change-management capacity, and the need to preserve accountable food-safety controls and qualified staff.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level capex plans, equipment age and controls inventories, OEE data, integrator quotations, validated pilot results, and interviews with operations and preventive-controls-qualified personnel at at least ten LMM 311824 plants would materially move the range.
caveats: No representative adoption survey for LMM US 311824 plants was found.
Five-year implementation depends on plant layout, customer audits, and whether existing lines expose usable sensor and production data.
Physical automation capex and conventional software are included only where AI enables the exposed task opportunity.

e.rationale: The code spans dry pasta, prepared mixes, and dough, and includes both branded consumer products and outsourced supply. A historical pasta manufacturer reported retail private-label and institutional channels, customer-specific packaging and material requirements, and food-processor ingredient customers, showing that eligible models are substantive but not universal. The estimate treats the qualifying unit as the firm, not an isolated contract, so firms need a material repeat outsourced-supply business and transferable operations.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the 83 dataset-estimated LMM candidates, classifying revenue by branded, private-label, ingredient, institutional, and customer-specific channels and screening for captive status and owner dependence, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The public filing that reveals channel mix is from 2007 and a large pasta producer, not the current LMM population.
NAICS data do not identify private-label or contract-manufacturing revenue at firm level.
The supplied n estimate is margin-bridged and may include firms whose EBITDA or business model does not meet the lens.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across US employer-business owners, while McKinsey combines Census and transaction datasets for small businesses and reports manufacturing-owner age. I use the 22% employer intention rate as an upper anchor, reduce it for noncompletion and nonqualifying internal transfers, and modestly offset that reduction because more than 60% of manufacturing owners are over 55. This may still overstate completed external control transfers because intentions are not closings.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years, while manufacturing ownership is older than the overall small-business population. Capital intensity, specialized equipment, working capital, and a thin sub-institutional buyer market impede completion, so the base sits below stated intentions despite elevated succession pressure.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal counts from PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, Axial, SBA 7(a), and proprietary food-industry broker records matched to 311824 and $1-10M EBITDA, plus owner-age and succession interviews across the candidate list, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes transfers by gift and rare public offerings, not only qualifying control acquisitions.
McKinsey's estimates use multiple third-party datasets and define small businesses as firms with fewer than 500 employees, broader than the lens.
No 311824-specific completed-transfer denominator was found.

q.bridge: A pasta-company filing documents cost pass-through terms and a UK competition authority study describes retailer bargaining and low own-label margins across food categories. I translate these contract and market structures into the discounted share of an AI-enabled gross benefit retained by a US LMM supplier. The older US filing and UK market evidence may not capture current US contract cadence; stronger retailer concentration would bias retention lower.
q.rationale: Contract manufacturers can initially keep operational savings, especially between formal price resets, but transparent input economics, customer-specific bids, renewals, retailer purchasing power, and competitive rebids share a substantial portion over five years. Pasta contracts have historically passed direct-material cost changes both up and down, and own-label suppliers compete strongly to retain retailer contracts.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized customer contracts and renewal histories showing open-book clauses, cost-down requirements, indexation, bid frequency, gross-margin bridges, and realized price concessions after productivity improvements would materially change retention.
caveats: The pasta filing is old and from a larger issuer; contract structures may have changed.
The competition study covers the UK and multiple own-label food categories rather than US 311824.
Branded products may retain more benefit than the outsourced private-label and institutional lens.

d5.bridge: USDA measures wheat-flour use and trade rather than constant-price demand for the exact 311824 outsourced service basket. I carry stable staple demand and population support into a near-flat five-year quantity path, then widen the downside for low-carbohydrate and gluten-free preferences and for imports of pasta and noodles. Because the code also includes non-pasta mixes and dough, wheat use is an imperfect demand proxy in both directions.
d5.rationale: USDA describes US wheat-flour food demand as relatively stable and largely insensitive to wheat prices or disposable income, but reports per-capita flour use of 126.6 pounds in 2025 after long-run pressure from low-carbohydrate and gluten-free preferences. Population and convenience formats can support total quantity, while imports and dietary substitution constrain domestic outsourced production.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NielsenIQ five-year unit-volume data for dry pasta, prepared flour mixes, and refrigerated or frozen dough by private-label and foodservice channel, combined with Census shipment quantities and import penetration, would materially change the range.
caveats: Wheat-flour disappearance is broader than 311824 and does not separate domestic production from imports.
The category mixes mature dry pasta with potentially different growth patterns in dough and specialty mixes.
Constant-quality unit measures are difficult where package sizes and formulations change.

o.bridge: FDA rules apply to registered human-food facilities rather than directly measuring retained operator share. I infer that physical food manufacturing, facility-specific hazard controls, monitoring, records, and accountable qualified oversight make full software substitution implausible. Contract loss to imports or customer insourcing is handled in demand, not here; the remaining uncertainty is limited self-manufacture or process elimination.
o.rationale: The output is physical food requiring mixing or extrusion, drying or temperature control, packaging, sanitation, maintenance, and distribution. FDA requires covered facilities to maintain facility-specific food-safety plans, preventive controls, monitoring, and qualified oversight. AI and automation can change staffing but do not remove the need for an accountable manufacturing operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, announced insourcing projects, adoption data for lights-out food plants, and FDA-recognized evidence on remotely supervised or autonomous preventive-control systems would materially change this value.
caveats: Some customers may insource narrow production runs or replace mixes with alternative formats.
The value is about the need for an operator, not the amount of labor inside the plant.
Small qualified-facility exemptions can modify some FDA requirements but do not eliminate physical production accountability.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent target is not every 311824 manufacturer but LMM private-label, ingredient, institutional, and customer-specific suppliers with repeat external relationships. They combine durable physical demand and transferable plant assets with a moderate AI opportunity concentrated in inspection, planning, administration, quality records, and production support rather than the core physical process.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve machine vision, anomaly detection, production scheduling, demand forecasting, order handling, quality-document review, and maintenance triage. The large production and material-moving workforce limits the exposed share, and food-safety validation, legacy controls, and customer audits slow implementation.
narrative.value_capture: Operational savings can remain with the supplier between bids and renewals, but private-label and institutional purchasing is price-sensitive. Open-book or indexed terms, transparent input economics, cost-down requests, and competitive rebids are likely to return a meaningful share to customers over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: The dataset estimates 83 firms in the EBITDA band, but only a subset has a material outsourced-supply model. Manufacturing owners are unusually old, creating succession pressure, while specialized equipment, working capital, and a limited small-deal buyer pool reduce the fraction of intentions that become completed qualifying transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Dry pasta and flour-based foods are mature staples with stable aggregate demand, supported by population and convenience, but per-capita wheat use faces dietary pressure and imported pasta competes with domestic output. Nearly all remaining quantity still needs an accountable physical operator even if plants automate further.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The strongest limitations are the absence of six-digit occupation and transfer data, the mixed pasta/mix/dough population, and sparse current public contract evidence. A target can also be unattractive if it is a commodity private-label supplier with concentrated customers, mandatory cost-downs, old equipment, weak food-safety systems, or branded revenue that falls outside the lens.
narrative.principal_driver: Stable physical food demand plus repeat customer-specific supply supports a durable operator role and recurring workflow-improvement opportunity.
narrative.principal_weakness: The AI-exposed share is modest and customer bargaining can erode savings, while the exact eligible and transferable firm population is not directly observed.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 311824 Dry Pasta, Dough, and Flour Mixes Manufacturing from Purchased Flour
sources.supports: Defines the industry's included manufacturing activities and cross-references, including dry pasta, prepared flour mixes, and dough from purchased flour.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311800
sources.supports: Provides the broader-industry occupation mix, including 49.70% production, 13.23% transportation/material moving, 5.24% office support, 6.71% packaging-machine operators, and 1.14% inspectors.

sources.title: Smart sensors meet spaghetti
sources.supports: Describes an operating pasta-line machine-vision system that recognizes defects in real time, rejects affected pasta, and reduces reliance on manual inspection.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

sources.title: Navigating the great small business ownership transition
sources.supports: Reports that more than 60% of owners in manufacturing, mining, and utilities are over 55, describes transfer frictions for capital-intensive firms, and estimates manufacturing ownership exits.

sources.title: American Italian Pasta Company Form 10-K
sources.supports: Documents retail private-label and institutional pasta channels, customer-specific requirements, food-processor customers, and contractual pass-through of direct-material cost changes in both directions.

sources.title: Wheat Sector at a Glance
sources.supports: States that US wheat-flour food demand is relatively stable, gives 2025 per-capita flour use of 126.6 pounds, discusses dietary pressure, and identifies pasta and noodles as major wheat-product imports.

sources.title: Price inflation and competition in food and grocery manufacturing and supply
sources.supports: Finds strong competition among own-label food manufacturers for retailer contracts, switching, transparent supplier costs, competitive retailer pricing, and generally low or declining supplier margins.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Requires covered food facilities to maintain hazard-based food-safety plans and preventive controls and describes binding employee qualification, training, verification, and monitoring obligations.
